nodes.name: Sticky Note2

parameters.content:
## 2. Use Prompt-Based Object Detection
[Read more about the HTTP node]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

We've had generalised object detection before ([see my other template using ResNet]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }} but being able to prompt for what you're looking for is a very exciting proposition! Not only could this reduce the effort in post-detection filtering but also introduce contextual use-cases such as searching by "emotion", "locality", "anomolies" and many more!

I found the the output json schema of `{ "box_2d": { "type": "array", ... } }` works best for Gemini to return coordinates.

nodes.notes: This stickyNote node performs automated tasks as part of the workflow.

### Q. Why not use the Basic LLM node?
At time of writing, Langchain version does not recognise Gemini 2.0 to be a multimodal model.

nodes.name: Sticky Note6

parameters.content:
## Try it out!
### This n8n template demonstrates how to use Gemini 2.0's new Bounding Box detection capabilities your workflows.

The key difference being this enables prompt-based object detection for images which is pretty powerful for things like contextual search over an image. eg. "Put a bounding box around all adults with children in this image" or "Put a bounding box around cars parked out of bounds of a parking space".

## How it works
* An image is downloaded via the HTTP node and an "Edit Image" node is used to extract the file's width and height.
* The image is then given to the Gemini 2.0 API to parse and return coordinates of the bounding box of the requested subjects. In this demo, we've asked for the AI to identify all bunnies.
* The coordinates are then rescaled with the original image's width and height to correctl align them.
* Finally to measure the accuracy of the object detection, we use the "Edit Image" node to draw the bounding boxes onto the original image.


### Need Help?
Join the [Discord]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }} or ask in the [Forum]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

Happy Hacking!

nodes.notes: This stickyNote node performs automated tasks as part of the workflow.

parameters.content:
## 2. Use Prompt-Based Object Detection
[Read more about the HTTP node]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

We've had generalised object detection before ([see my other template using ResNet]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }} but being able to prompt for what you're looking for is a very exciting proposition! Not only could this reduce the effort in post-detection filtering but also introduce contextual use-cases such as searching by "emotion", "locality", "anomolies" and many more!

I found the the output json schema of `{ "box_2d": { "type": "array", ... } }` works best for Gemini to return coordinates.

### Q. Why not use the Basic LLM node?
At time of writing, Langchain version does not recognise Gemini 2.0 to be a multimodal model.

## Try it out!
### This n8n template demonstrates how to use Gemini 2.0's new Bounding Box detection capabilities your workflows.

The key difference being this enables prompt-based object detection for images which is pretty powerful for things like contextual search over an image. eg. "Put a bounding box around all adults with children in this image" or "Put a bounding box around cars parked out of bounds of a parking space".

## How it works
* An image is downloaded via the HTTP node and an "Edit Image" node is used to extract the file's width and height.
* The image is then given to the Gemini 2.0 API to parse and return coordinates of the bounding box of the requested subjects. In this demo, we've asked for the AI to identify all bunnies.
* The coordinates are then rescaled with the original image's width and height to correctl align them.
* Finally to measure the accuracy of the object detection, we use the "Edit Image" node to draw the bounding boxes onto the original image.


### Need Help?
Join the [Discord]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }} or ask in the [Forum]({{ $env.WEBHOOK_URL }}

Happy Hacking!

prerequisites: An active n8n instance installed locally, in a Docker container, or hosted on n8n Cloud.
Basic familiarity with importing JSON templates in n8n.
setupInstructions: Download the JSON configuration for this workflow.
Ensure you set up credentials for all connected integrations.
Test your trigger and run execution.
requiredCredentials: google Palm  A P I details
configurationSteps: Verify the settings of the trigger node "When clicking ‘Test workflow’".
Open the "Get Test Image" node (n8n-nodes-base.httpRequest) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Gemini 2.0 Object Detection" node (n8n-nodes-base.httpRequest) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Draw Bounding Boxes" node (n8n-nodes-base.editImage) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Get Image Info" node (n8n-nodes-base.editImage) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note1" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note2" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note3" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note4" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note5" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Open the "Sticky Note6" node (n8n-nodes-base.stickyNote) and configure the action parameters, mappings, and fields.
Locate the Set/Edit fields node "Get Variables".
Define or edit values for variables that are referenced in downstream nodes.
testingSteps: Click the "Test step" or "Listen for test event" button on your Trigger node.
Send a test trigger from your source service (e.g. fill a test form or trigger a webhook trigger).
Verify that the node canvas lights up green, indicating successful data flow.
Open the final destination node to confirm that output records were saved correctly.
instructions.expectedResult: Upon trigger activation, inputs will flow through the configured nodes and execute actions successfully.
troubleshooting: Check credential warning symbols. Most workflow issues stem from expired OAuth connections or missing API permissions.
Verify that the data keys coming from your Trigger node match the exact names mapped in subsequent nodes (e.g., {{ $json.body.email }}).
Open the Execution history tab in n8n to view logs and determine which node failed.
securityNotes: Never hardcode API keys, passwords, or authentication bearer tokens in the plain text fields of node parameters.
If you download or share this workflow JSON, sanitize any sensitive parameters or configuration nodes.
customizationIdeas: Add an email node at the end to notify you of successful executions.
Incorporate an AI LLM node to summarize trigger payloads before writing them to integrations.
